turns.zh: Stripe 处理着全球许多增长最快企业的海量复杂业务，包括福布斯 AI50 中 78% 的公司和超过一半的财富 100 强。像 Atlassian、Figma 和 Urban Outfitters 这样的企业使用 Stripe 创建完全品牌化和定制化的结账页面，支持超过 125 种全球支付方式。我邀请 Stripe 的高管来这个播客的次数比其他任何公司都多，这是有原因的。他们知道如何构建既规模化又受人喜爱的优秀产品。而且 Stripe 远不止支付。他们还拥有领先的计费解决方案和高度优化的结账体验，专门用于提高结账转化率。加入 Salesforce、OpenAI 和 Pepsi 等行业领导者的行列，他们正在使用 Stripe 加速增长并推动全球 GDP 增长。访问 stripe.com 了解 Stripe 如何帮助您的业务增长。

turns.en: Speaking of moving fast and not waiting too long, let's talk about Figma Make. For people that don't know what Figma Make is, you've mentioned it a couple times, but just what's the simplest way to understand what is Figma Make?
turns.zh: 说到快速行动、不要等太久，我们来聊聊 Figma Make。对于那些不知道 Figma Make 是什么的人，你提到过几次，但最简单的理解方式是什么？

turns.en: How do you put it in a prompt and really easily get your idea onto a prototype that you can actually share and use with your team? And how do you go also to a working application that you can ship, put on the web, or use internally to speed up your workflows. The ways that people have both upleveled craft on the side of design by exploring more dynamic prototyping, but also how they've been able to create prototypes when normally they wouldn't otherwise—in the case of, for example, product managers—has been really interesting, at least in our team but also in many of our customers that we're visiting and talking with. It really changes the process once you have the ability to explore this option space in a bigger way. And PMs are no longer saying to the designer, "Hey, can you draw this thing out for me?" That frees up designer time to go explore more deeply the stuff they need to go into, and it allows anyone to kind of add to that first conversation of where should we go and look further and wider and broader at the option space. So yeah, I think it's something that is a top priority for us, and it's also something that we're rapidly improving. I mean, yesterday we launched a feature: once you take a screen from Figma Make, bring it into Figma Design, because sometimes the right thing to do is to prompt your way with iteration, and sometimes you just want to get in the details and actually tweak things, and you need to do it by hand to get exactly what you want. Then you've got to bring that context right back into Figma Make. So making that round trip happen is incredibly important. And so much more we're going to do from an interoperability standpoint to make it that you can go further, iterate faster, because the Make is really just a starting point when you have an AI output. Usually that's not where you end up.
turns.zh: 如何通过一个提示，非常轻松地将你的想法变成一个可以实际分享并与团队一起使用的原型？又如何进一步变成一个可以发布、放到网上或内部使用以加速工作流程的可用应用？人们既通过探索更动态的原型设计来提升设计工艺，也能够在原本不会创建原型的情况下（例如产品经理）创建原型——这在我们团队以及我们拜访和交谈的许多客户中都很有趣。一旦你能够以更大的方式探索这个选项空间，它确实改变了流程。产品经理不再对设计师说：“嘿，你能帮我画出这个东西吗？”这解放了设计师的时间，让他们可以更深入地探索需要深入的东西，并且允许任何人为最初的讨论——我们应该往哪个方向走——做出贡献，更广泛地审视选项空间。所以，我认为这是我们的首要任务，也是我们正在快速改进的事情。我的意思是，昨天我们发布了一个功能：一旦你从 Figma Make 中获取一个屏幕，将其导入 Figma Design，因为有时正确的做法是通过提示进行迭代，有时你只想深入细节并实际调整，你需要手动操作才能得到你想要的东西。然后你必须将该上下文带回 Figma Make。所以实现这种往返非常重要。从互操作性的角度来看，我们还将做更多事情，让你走得更远、迭代更快，因为当你获得 AI 输出时，Make 只是一个起点。通常那不是你的终点。

turns.en: Okay, cool. I definitely want to talk about that, but I'll just share I was playing with Figma Make the past week. I asked it to just clone Figma the app, and it's very good. All right. So, I'm going to launch a competitor. I think—
turns.zh: 好的，太酷了。我肯定想聊聊这个，但我先分享一下，过去一周我一直在玩 Figma Make。我让它克隆 Figma 应用本身，结果非常好。好吧，所以我要推出一个竞争对手了。我想——

turns.en: Watch out.
turns.zh: 小心了。

turns.en: I should try that prompt again. I mean, we made Make a lot better since I last tested it. So, it's legit. I'm making squares and circles and changing colors and fonts, and it's legit. I even added—like I was like, "Update the branding to look more like Figma," and it worked. And then I made a landing page for a Dylan and Lenny podcast episode, and it was—
turns.zh: 我应该再试试那个提示。我的意思是，自从我上次测试以来，我们把 Make 改进很多了。所以，它很靠谱。我在画正方形和圆形，改变颜色和字体，它很靠谱。我甚至加了——比如我说，“更新品牌风格，让它更像 Figma”，它做到了。然后我制作了一个 Dylan 和 Lenny 播客节目的着陆页，它——

turns.en: It can't—I was like, "Make the photos of us the real photos," but I think probably for copyright reasons it couldn't do that.
turns.zh: 它不能——我当时说，“把我们的照片换成真实的照片”，但可能因为版权原因它做不到。

turns.en: Well, you can also tweak the code. So I mean you can go in and put in custom images.
turns.zh: 嗯，你也可以调整代码。所以你可以进去放入自定义图片。

turns.en: Too much, that's too much work for me, Dylan. It's too much work.
turns.zh: 太多了，对我来说工作量太大了，Dylan。工作量太大了。

turns.en: Okay, just—you go to the point tool and then point edit, and then you can go directly to code on the right, and then you can just replace the URL. And just to FYI—
turns.zh: 好吧，你只需——进入点工具，然后点编辑，然后你可以直接转到右侧的代码，然后你只需替换 URL。另外，仅供参考——

turns.en: Okay, I love this live support we're doing. I see it. Okay. Okay. I'm going to do it. I'll link to it in the show notes.
turns.zh: 好的，我喜欢我们正在做的这种实时支持。我看到了。好的。好的。我会去做的。我会在节目笔记中链接到它。

turns.en: Let me follow the thread you just had here. So, right now, the use cases that seem to be emerging in this world of AI app prototyping or like prototypes for product teams. There's building real production apps. That seems to be one. Another is just like you said, designing, thinking through ideas, and then moving it to Figma and then building something. Where do you see Figma Make in that? And where do you think this evolves over time? Do you think these apps end up in this space just being like, "Here's how people will build product in the future," or do you think prototyping and internal tools—I think is the other one—do you think that's where it ends up being mostly?
turns.zh: 让我顺着你刚才的思路。那么，现在在 AI 应用原型设计或产品团队原型设计这个领域，似乎出现了一些用例。有构建真实生产应用，这似乎是一个。另一个就像你说的，设计、构思想法，然后将其移到 Figma 中，再构建东西。你认为 Figma Make 在其中处于什么位置？你认为随着时间的推移，它会如何演变？你认为这些应用最终会停留在“这就是人们未来构建产品的方式”这个领域，还是你认为原型设计和内部工具——我认为是另一个——你认为那会是主要方向？

turns.en: I think it's going to be very widespread across companies. The ability to go create prototypes in software, and I think it's a great thing. And it still takes a lot to go from an idea or a prototype or, you know, some internal tool that's not very polished to something that you're proud of.
turns.zh: 我认为它将在公司中非常普及。在软件中创建原型的能力，我认为这是一件好事。但从一个想法、一个原型或一个不太完善的内部工具，到一个你引以为豪的东西，仍然需要付出很多努力。

turns.en: Um and so I think this is additive to the design process, brings more people in, brings more context in around business constraints, but also still requires quite a lot of iteration, refinement, and that loop is so important to get right too. But yeah, our first mission that we have to accomplish and, you know, do in an incredible way is making it awesome for the prototyping case. But the second one that we're also working on and um I'd say it's again second to the prototyping case but so important is how do I go to something that's actually working and um that could be for you know a more robust prototype it could be for something you ship and actually build a business around or it could be an internal tool and um all those are interesting use cases and all of them have relevance for the wider company but uh prototyping is where we're really start making sure that we are awesome at another thing to mention is I think it's super important that people are able to use their design system and be consistent in Figma make and so we're putting a lot of effort into that. Uh right now um I'd say it's still in an earlier phase than we want. Um, we have a lot more we want to do here and that you'll see us do here.
turns.zh: 嗯，所以我认为这对设计流程是补充性的，能让更多人参与进来，带来更多关于业务约束的背景信息，但同时也仍然需要大量的迭代和打磨，这个循环也非常重要。不过，我们的首要任务——并且要以令人惊叹的方式完成——是让原型设计场景变得出色。但我们也在做的第二个任务，我再说一遍，它次于原型设计场景但同样重要，就是如何让你得到一个真正可用的东西，它可能是一个更健壮的原型，也可能是你发布并围绕它建立业务的东西，或者是一个内部工具。所有这些用例都很有趣，并且对整个公司都有意义，但原型设计是我们真正开始确保自己卓越的地方。另外要提的一点是，我认为让人们能够使用自己的设计系统并在 Figma Make 中保持一致性非常重要，所以我们在这方面投入了很多精力。嗯，目前我可以说它还处于比我们期望更早的阶段。我们还有很多想做的事情，你们会看到我们在这里继续推进。

turns.en: And, uh, it's, I think, critical that ideas don't die on the vine because you've got, um, a visual expression that doesn't match what everyone else expects. Sometimes people will just filter them out because they don't look right. If you can actually start with something that's consistent, um the idea then gets evaluated on its merits rather than it being oh yeah well you're used to like oh the wrong elements doesn't look quite right. Along those lines a lot of the a lot of the AI building apps all kind of look alike and everyone's just getting tired of seeing those sorts of products and being Figma being at the forefront of design. Is there anything you've done differently in how you create this product to make the designs really look really good and different?
turns.zh: 而且，我认为关键在于，想法不能因为视觉表达不符合大家的预期而夭折。有时人们会因为看起来不对就直接过滤掉它们。如果你能从一个一致的东西开始，那么想法就会根据其优点被评估，而不是“哦，是的，你习惯的，哦，元素不对，看起来不太对”。沿着这个思路，很多 AI 构建应用看起来都差不多，每个人都厌倦了看到这类产品，而 Figma 处于设计前沿。你们在创建这个产品时，有没有采取什么不同的做法，让设计看起来真正出色且与众不同？

turns.en: Yeah, I mean making sure that we have incredible quality with visual outputs that is super important to us obviously. So uh that's something that we're constantly thinking about and working on much more. Uh but that's really
turns.zh: 是的，我的意思是确保我们在视觉输出上拥有令人难以置信的质量，这对我们来说显然非常重要。所以这是我们一直在思考并投入更多精力的事情。嗯，但这确实……

turns.en: and uh mysterious.
turns.zh: 而且有点神秘。

turns.en: Well, it also just I think the fact that it lives within the platform is very important too because that unlocks more opportunity to make it so that we can make it interoperable with the rest of the platform. Bringing stuff from make into Figma design completing that loop but also exposing make and all the other places that can live. Uh we're very excited with that. And then MCP as well making it so that you can go uh use MCP to pull for make. um make is shouldn't be the only end destination. Uh we need to create an ecosystem uh that that talks to other ecosystems and so we've been putting a lot of effort into our MCP in general and that includes me too.
turns.zh: 嗯，而且我认为它存在于平台内部这一事实也非常重要，因为这解锁了更多机会，让我们可以使其与平台的其他部分互操作。将 Make 中的东西带入 Figma 设计，完成这个循环，同时也将 Make 暴露到所有其他可以存在的地方。我们对此非常兴奋。还有 MCP，让你可以使用 MCP 来拉取 Make。Make 不应该是唯一的终点。我们需要创建一个与其他生态系统对话的生态系统，所以我们总体上在 MCP 上投入了很多精力，这也包括我。

turns.en: I saw you guys uh topped a leaderboard. You tweeted some uh research report. What was that about?
turns.zh: 我看到你们登上了排行榜榜首。你发推文提到了一份研究报告。那是关于什么的？

turns.en: It was really cool. It was like someone had done uh basically a academic paper on okay what is the right way to compare different outputs and I was pleased to see that we came out I think it was second to the top so still work to do and yeah it's exciting and cool to see Figma make in an academic paper uh that was that was a new one for me I don't usually see the the academic literature measurement products
turns.zh: 那真的很酷。基本上有人做了一篇学术论文，研究什么是比较不同输出的正确方法，我很高兴看到我们排名第二，所以还有工作要做。看到 Figma Make 出现在学术论文中很令人兴奋和酷，这对我来说是第一次，我通常不怎么看学术文献中的产品评测。

turns.en: what was the what were they how were they approaching and what not
turns.zh: 他们是怎么做的？他们的方法是什么？

turns.en: pairwise comparison mostly I'm not saying that's like the perfect way it requires a lot of intention about who was doing the pair pair wise comparison too um but yeah visual output is something that we really care about for make and
turns.zh: 主要是成对比较。我不是说这是完美的方法，它也需要对谁在做成对比较有很深的考量。嗯，但视觉输出确实是我们在 Make 中非常关心的。

turns.en: so it was like which of these is a better design is that was that what that research was looking at or better output or more correct output
turns.zh: 所以那项研究是在看哪个设计更好，还是输出更好或更正确？

turns.en: yeah and I think starting points just really matter so if you can get people to the right starting point sooner that's extraordinarily helpful And there's a lot of ways to help people do that.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为起点真的很重要。所以如果你能让人更快地到达正确的起点，那会非常有帮助。有很多方法可以帮助人们做到这一点。

ts.sec: Lessons from the Make Design launch

turns.en: I want to talk about when you guys first launched your AI product. This was actually the year of Config when I when I interviewed you at Config. I remember you got you were very distracted because there was the reaction wasn't amazing.
turns.zh: 我想谈谈你们第一次推出 AI 产品的时候。那实际上是在 Config 大会那年，我在 Config 采访你时。我记得你当时很分心，因为反应不太好。

turns.en: It actually came a little bit after our interview, but I do think I was exhausted by the time we did that interview. So, apologies.
turns.zh: 实际上那是在我们采访之后不久发生的，但我确实觉得做那次采访时我已经筋疲力尽了。所以，抱歉。

turns.en: I imagine that was a long day and our interviews right at the end. What So, what happened with that launch? I know you guys had to pull some stuff back. I imagine taught you a lot. What What happened? What' you learn?
turns.zh: 我想那是漫长的一天，我们的采访正好在最后。那么，那次发布发生了什么？我知道你们不得不撤回一些东西。我想这让你学到了很多。发生了什么？你学到了什么？

turns.en: So, we had this feature that internally we called first draft and for some reason we changed the name to make design which first of all by the way wrong name. We never intended it to be like here's your design you're done. Uh it was really a starting point and we knew that and um this was early on in our sort of AI journey and the approach was basically um nothing with fancy training or uh you know like user data. It was all about okay you've got an LM assembling legal pieces and uh doing that according to a prompt. So, it's very basic in the way we built it and it could get you choose some pretty cool outputs and you could edit the outputs and change you know colors, typography, uh some of the parts of the theme and uh I think that the industry then even though it wasn't that long ago was in a very different place in terms of the conversation around AI than we are today but also uh people put us through its paces in ways that you know we hadn't fully done. And one of the things they found uh was that if you typed in make me a weather app, it would make you something that looked pretty much similar to the Apple weather app. And given that that was under our control and that was really about, you know, we should have had better QA uh and really looked at all the subcomponents more closely. Um, uh, I felt like, you know, maybe I would have felt differently if it was we had trained this model and now we got to, you know, tweak some of the the ways that we're post- training or whatever. But with the approach we were using, I was like, this was preventable. This is a QA failure. And so I pulled it. Uh, it was actually during our second config because we did the main one and then we went to Singapore and did a second. And if I was tired during your last the last podcast we did together, I was even more tired then because uh the Singapore time zone shift is brutal uh from SF. And so uh yeah, I'm sure we could have had better communication about the way we did it. But I thought it was the right thing to do. Uh would have done the same thing if you teleported me back and um uh and we were interested after we did a lot of QA.
turns.zh: 我们有一个内部称为“初稿”的功能，但出于某种原因我们把它改名为“Make Design”，首先，顺便说一句，这个名字不对。我们从未打算让它成为“这就是你的设计，你完成了”。它实际上是一个起点，我们知道这一点。这在我们 AI 之旅的早期，方法基本上没有花哨的训练或用户数据。就是有一个语言模型根据提示组装合法元素。所以构建方式非常基础，它可以让你得到一些很酷的输出，你可以编辑输出，改变颜色、排版、主题的一些部分。我认为当时的行业，尽管时间并不久远，在围绕 AI 的讨论方面与今天截然不同，但人们以我们尚未完全经历的方式对我们进行了考验。他们发现的一件事是，如果你输入“给我做一个天气应用”，它会做出一个看起来很像苹果天气应用的东西。考虑到这在我们控制之下，这实际上是我们本应有更好的 QA，更仔细地检查所有子组件。嗯，我觉得，如果我们训练了这个模型，然后我们需要调整一些后训练的方式，我可能会有不同的感受。但以我们使用的方法，我认为这是可以预防的。这是 QA 的失败。所以我把它撤回了。实际上那是在我们的第二次 Config 大会期间，因为我们做了主大会，然后去了新加坡做第二次。如果我在我们上次一起做的播客中很累，那当时我更累，因为新加坡的时差从旧金山过去很残酷。所以，是的，我确定我们本可以更好地沟通我们做事的方式。但我认为那是正确的做法。如果把我传送回去，我也会做同样的事情，而且我们在做了大量 QA 之后才重新上线。

turns.en: And so, I think that maybe takeaways from that, first of all, you got to put it through its paces, especially when you've got a wide surface area that can be explored through something like this. And you really have to understand what are the inputs? Make sure to do the QA work and push the product and the team to hold up that high bar. How do you actually do this QA work? This is a big problem for a lot of AI companies these days. They're just so non-deterministic. There's all this autonomy you got to give them. How do you do this? Is this like you work with someone else that does a bunch of work for you or is it a team that just is really good at AI QA?
turns.zh: 所以，我想从中得到的启示是，首先，你必须对它进行充分测试，尤其是当它涉及一个可以通过这种方式探索的广阔领域时。你真的需要理解输入是什么？确保做好 QA 工作，并推动产品和团队保持高标准。你实际上是如何做这些 QA 工作的？这是如今很多 AI 公司面临的大问题。它们太不确定了，你必须给它们很多自主权。你是怎么做的？是和其他人合作，让他们为你做大量工作，还是有一个非常擅长 AI QA 的团队？

turns.en: We have done a lot of work to figure out how we do eval and we're also continuing to evolve our process. So yeah, it's something that you have to be really focused on and I think that it's easy to go on vibes for too long. Some folks just kind of trust the vibes and that'll get you somewhere, but it's not rigorous.
turns.zh: 我们做了大量工作来弄清楚如何进行评估，并且我们也在不断改进我们的流程。所以，是的，这是你必须非常专注的事情，我认为人们很容易长时间凭感觉行事。有些人就是相信感觉，这能让你有所进展，但不够严谨。

turns.en: Awesome. We've had a lot of episodes on eval so essentially what I'm hearing is just getting good at eval is the solution to avoiding those problems.
turns.zh: 太棒了。我们有很多关于评估的节目，所以我听到的本质上就是，擅长评估是避免这些问题的解决方案。

turns.en: Part of the solution. Yes.
turns.zh: 解决方案的一部分。是的。

turns.en: Part of the solution. Going back to Make, just so people have this mental model in their head of when they think about other folks in the space that they're aware of, is there a way you're positioning Make that is different or is the idea eventually they all will kind of be prototypes, internal tools, full production apps, or do you think about it differently where Make is going?
turns.zh: 解决方案的一部分。回到 Make，为了让人们在脑海中有一个心理模型，当他们想到这个领域里他们知道的其他人时，你定位 Make 的方式是否有所不同？还是说最终它们都会成为原型、内部工具、完整的生产应用？或者你对 Make 的发展方向有不同的看法？

turns.en: You know, if you just kind of zoom out and again it's what's the bigger point here? If you want to win in the game of software, you need to differentiate through design. Like that's again how you win or lose. Craft matters. And so we're no longer in this era of good enough is fine. It's like good enough is not enough. It's mediocre. You got to get to great if you want to win. Preferably excellent. And I think that with Figma Make, the more we can do to help you get to a great starting point then also iterate refine from there towards something excellent and also go wide explore the option space. There's a lot we can do that I think will be very very differentiated and some of that's already there, some is coming, and this is I think the fastest we ever evolved a product surface so I've been really proud of how fast we've been able to grow Figma Make's abilities and also just make it more and more excellent for our users. Still on that journey and we're always improving but you will see things in the next weeks months in terms of what we're shipping and the progress will continue to accelerate.
turns.zh: 你知道，如果你退一步看，更大的意义是什么？如果你想在软件游戏中获胜，你需要通过设计来差异化。这又是你如何赢或输的关键。工艺很重要。所以我们不再处于“足够好就行”的时代。足够好已经不够了，那是平庸。如果你想赢，你必须做到优秀，最好是卓越。我认为对于 Figma Make 来说，我们越能帮助你达到一个优秀的起点，然后从那里迭代改进到卓越，同时广泛探索选项空间，我们能做的就越多。我认为这将是非常差异化的，其中一些已经存在，一些即将到来，而且我认为这是我们产品表面进化最快的一次，所以我非常自豪我们能够如此快速地提升 Figma Make 的能力，并让它对我们的用户越来越出色。我们仍在路上，一直在改进，但在接下来的几周和几个月里，你会看到我们发布的东西，进步会继续加速。

turns.en: Fascinating. So what I'm hearing essentially is the opportunity you see is making great excellent well-designed experiences, things that are not just good.
turns.zh: 太迷人了。所以我听到的本质上就是，你看到的机会是创造优秀、卓越、设计精良的体验，不仅仅是好的东西。

turns.en: I think it's what you have to do across the board if you want to win.
turns.zh: 我认为如果你想赢，这是你必须全面做到的。

turns.en: Such a cool thing. I'm so excited to see how you guys do this. This connects to something I wanted to ask about that I skipped, but I'm excited to come back to it. This idea of taste. You talk a lot about the importance of taste in developing great products. It's something people hear, they're like, 'What the hell is taste? Do I have taste? I don't know.' How would you describe just like, 'What is taste? What's the simplest way for someone to understand taste?' And is there like a test that you find is helpful for people to see if they actually have good taste? Something that's like, 'I actually don't know what you're talking about.'
turns.zh: 真酷。我很兴奋想看看你们怎么做。这联系到我想问但跳过的一个问题，我很高兴能回到它。就是品味这个概念。你经常谈到品味在开发优秀产品中的重要性。人们听到这个会想，‘品味到底是什么？我有品味吗？我不知道。’你会怎么描述，‘什么是品味？让人理解品味最简单的方式是什么？’有没有一种测试，你觉得对人们判断自己是否有好品味有帮助？就像，‘我真的不知道你在说什么。’

turns.en: Maybe a taste test.
turns.zh: 也许是个品味测试。

turns.en: A taste test. Exactly. Uh I think starting with taste, there's a million definitions of taste just like design, but I come back to like what's your point of view on things and how do you develop your point of view? I think there's some people maybe are born with stronger preferences about everything. Some folks don't care as much. They're not as intentional, but anyone can definitely lean into this. It's just this loop of okay, I'm having an experience of any sense. Maybe I'm looking at art, maybe I'm hearing music, maybe I'm literally eating food and tasting something, but do I like it? Do I not like it? Why? Okay, now go further, build your repertoire. Understand what is the greater context. What is the canon that led to this thing? And where do you disagree or agree philosophically with the path that brought everyone there? And I think the more you go through this loop and the more you're exposed to, the more you can refine your taste. And I don't think that leads everyone to becoming a taste maker. I think that is a 0.01% skill to be a true taste maker to be able to interpolate between the different directions people have explored historically or expand into something that's brand new. Not everyone's going to go create a new genre of literature or not everyone's going to be like Kurt Cobain or fundamentally find a new aesthetic or new art movement. But I think that for those who can create and then articulate a framework around what is taste for us, that is really an important skill. And then I think people can a lot of people can basically match a framework, not many people can create the framework.
turns.zh: 品味测试。没错。我认为从品味开始，品味有无数种定义，就像设计一样，但我回到的是，你对事物的观点是什么，以及你如何发展你的观点？我认为有些人可能天生对一切都有更强的偏好。有些人没那么在意，他们不那么刻意，但任何人都可以投入其中。这只是一个循环：好吧，我正在经历某种感官体验。也许我在看艺术，也许我在听音乐，也许我 literally 在吃东西品尝味道，但，我喜欢吗？我不喜欢吗？为什么？好，现在更进一步，建立你的知识库。理解更大的背景是什么。导致这件事的经典是什么？你在哲学上同意或不同意那条把大家带到这里的路径？我认为你经历这个循环越多，接触得越多，你就越能磨练你的品味。我不认为这会让每个人都成为品味创造者。我认为成为真正的品味创造者是一个 0.01% 的技能，能够插值人们历史上探索过的不同方向，或者扩展到全新的东西。不是每个人都会去创造一种新的文学体裁，或者不是每个人都会像 Kurt Cobain 那样，或者从根本上找到一种新的美学或新的艺术运动。但我认为，对于那些能够创造并阐明一个关于“品味对我们来说是什么”的框架的人来说，这确实是一项重要的技能。然后我认为很多人基本上可以匹配一个框架，但没多少人能创造这个框架。

turns.en: Wow that is such an incredible answer. So let me follow up here. One is just is there some kind of taste test that you find of like here's okay this person has great taste and then your point is you can develop this even if you don't start. So how what's one tip for someone that wants to develop their taste?
turns.zh: 哇，这个回答太棒了。那么让我接着问。第一，有没有某种品味测试，比如，好吧，这个人有很好的品味？而你的观点是，即使你起点不高，也可以培养品味。那么，对于想培养品味的人，你有什么建议？

turns.en: I think again it's just the more you can expand your viewpoints by looking at new things like finding the cross correlations, the links between different areas and different fields, different mediums, the better. And I think then reflecting on why, creating framework for yourself, just building that internal curatorial ability is very important. And I think yeah, how do you like look at every expression of human creativity that you can, be curious, learn, and then refine your own thinking, your own viewpoints, be willing to revisit the ones you've had in the past. That's what leads to great taste. And there is something about judgment in there too. Implied in taste is that some things are good and some things are bad. So I think you have to be willing to lean into that yourself in terms of being high judgment. Then also, I think the best designers on the product side can turn it on and off. They can go, I have my own taste. I know what I like. And then, okay, you're going for this. And that might be different than what I like, but I can match it brand as well.
turns.zh: 我认为还是，你越能通过观察新事物来扩展你的观点，比如寻找不同领域、不同学科、不同媒介之间的交叉关联和联系，就越好。然后反思原因，为自己建立框架，培养那种内在的策展能力，这非常重要。而且，是的，你如何尽可能地去审视人类创造力的每一种表达，保持好奇，学习，然后完善你自己的思考和观点，愿意重新审视你过去的观点。这就是通往伟大品味的道路。其中也涉及判断力。品味隐含的意思是，有些东西是好的，有些是坏的。所以我认为你必须愿意自己在这方面投入，保持高判断力。此外，我认为产品方面最好的设计师可以开关这种能力。他们可以想，我有自己的品味，我知道我喜欢什么。然后，好吧，你追求这个。这可能和我的喜好不同，但我也能匹配这个品牌。

ts.sec: People with great taste at Figma

turns.en: Not to put you on the spot, but is there someone that comes to mind when you think of this person has great taste that maybe isn't an obvious, you know, like a Steve Jobs, maybe another leader? I don't know, someone that this won't be an exhaustive list of all people that have amazing taste, but just anyone come to mind.
turns.zh: 不是要让你为难，但说到品味很好的人，你脑海里会不会浮现出某个人，可能不是那种显而易见的，比如史蒂夫·乔布斯，也许是另一位领导者？我不知道，不需要是品味非凡者的完整名单，只是随便想到谁都可以。

turns.en: A lot of people with great taste at Figma. I'm very lucky. I'll list a few. I think Damian, our creative director, Merchin on our product design team, Amber, our editor, but also one person we've recently hired that I think has incredible taste is Laura Donna. She's our new chief design officer. Just came over from Meta and still getting to know her in the Figma context. I think this is her fourth day, recorded on the 26th of September, but already I've just seen so many examples where her taste is really, really strong. And it's interesting actually she grew up as a musician and then went into the field of design. So going back to that cross-area, cross-field, discipline connectivity, I definitely think there's something to that.
turns.zh: Figma 有很多品味很好的人。我很幸运。我列举几个。我觉得是我们的创意总监 Damian，产品设计团队的 Merchin，我们的编辑 Amber，还有我们最近聘用的一位品味极佳的人，Laura Donna。她是我们的新任首席设计官，刚从 Meta 过来，还在 Figma 的环境中了解她。我想今天是她的第四天，录制于 9 月 26 日，但我已经看到很多例子，她的品味真的非常强。有趣的是，她从小是音乐家，后来进入设计领域。所以回到那种跨领域、跨学科的连接性，我绝对认为这里面有某种道理。

turns.en: To that point, it's wild how many people on this podcast were very serious musicians before they got into business and product. Like a lot of piano players, I'm noticing.
turns.zh: 说到这一点，这个播客里有多少人在进入商业和产品之前是非常认真的音乐家，真是不可思议。比如很多钢琴演奏者，我注意到了。

turns.en: Yep.
turns.zh: 是的。

ts.sec: Future of product development in 5 years

turns.en: Maybe a final question before we get to a very exciting lightning round. If you're just to think about how product development will look in the future, say in five or 10 years, 10 years, let's forget that that's too long. Say in five years, what do you think that looks like? What do you think will be most different in how people build product and build companies?
turns.zh: 也许在进入非常精彩的快问快答环节之前，最后一个问题。如果你想想未来产品开发会是什么样子，比如五年或十年，十年太长了，我们忘掉它。就说五年，你觉得会是什么样？你认为人们构建产品和公司的方式最大的不同会是什么？

turns.en: The trend that we've been seeing for the past 5 years is a trend that's going to accelerate the next 5 years, and that's a shift in emerging of roles. I just think that we're seeing more designers, engineers, product managers, researchers, all these different folks that are involved in the product development process dip their toe into the roles. We actually did some research around this. It was pretty interesting to see the results. So like 72% of respondents said AI-powered tools are one of the top reasons behind the expansion of roles and responsibilities. And I think part of that is that AI makes everyone feel the need to be more of a generalist too. There's kind of a meta there which is interesting. 56% of non-designers said that they engaged a great deal in at least one design-centric task like prototyping or visual brand exploration. We had actually done that question a year before with a similar respondent set and it was up 12 percentage points from a year ago, so from 44 to 56%. And 53% of respondents said that they agree that even with AI you still need deep knowledge to do a task well, which I thought was fascinating. That 53% both indicates that I think there's some amount of okay, you can do something with AI and be done, which I think might be wrong, but also an impulse towards more generalist abilities and the willingness to go dip your toe in new waters. So the takeaway is role boundaries will merge and it'll be less engineer, design, PM. It'll be people do many things. We're all product builders and some of us are specialized in our particular area.
turns.zh: 过去五年我们看到的趋势将在未来五年加速，那就是角色的融合与演变。我认为我们看到越来越多的设计师、工程师、产品经理、研究人员等参与产品开发过程的人开始涉足其他角色。我们实际上做了一些相关研究，结果很有趣。72% 的受访者表示 AI 驱动的工具是角色和职责扩展的首要原因之一。我认为部分原因是 AI 让每个人都觉得需要变得更像通才。这里面有一种元层面的东西，很有意思。56% 的非设计师表示他们大量参与了至少一项以设计为中心的任务，比如原型设计或视觉品牌探索。我们一年前用类似的受访者群体问过同样的问题，这个比例从 44% 上升到了 56%，上升了 12 个百分点。53% 的受访者表示他们同意即使有了 AI，你仍然需要深厚知识才能做好一项任务，我觉得这很吸引人。这个 53% 既表明我认为有一部分人觉得可以用 AI 做点事然后完事——这可能是错的——也表明了一种向通才能力发展的冲动和涉足新领域的意愿。所以结论是角色边界将融合，不再那么泾渭分明地分为工程师、设计师、产品经理。人们会做很多事情。我们都是产品构建者，有些人在特定领域有专长。

turns.en: Oh, I love that. I've been using the word product builder a lot more actually too. It just feels like such a better term for set a product manager or engineer. There's this question of will which function will be most taken on by other functions? For example, do you think like engineers and PMs will become engineers and designers will become more PM-y, PMs will become more design-y? Like which function maybe is most in trouble is one way to put it.
turns.zh: 哦，我喜欢这个说法。我自己实际上也更常用“产品构建者”这个词。感觉这比产品经理或工程师好多了。有一个问题是，哪个职能最容易被其他职能承担？比如，你认为工程师和产品经理会变成工程师，设计师会变得更像产品经理，产品经理会变得更像设计师？哪种职能可能最受冲击，这是一种问法。

turns.en: I think that it all depends on the way that things play out from here. Of course, no one knows if we're on an S-curve of progress or an exponential curve, or actually we're on the end of the S-curve but it's about to become exponential because a new architecture breaks through. I think the only thing that we know is that models will improve. Will it be incremental? Will it be exponential? I mean somewhere in between. Who knows? But what you have to believe is that you get better as models get better. Your organization gets better as models get better. And right now at least we are nowhere near, at least in Figma, the point where our demand for development for example is satiated. Have we seen productivity increases? Yeah, mild to moderate, but that is not something that has made our new headcount we want for engineering go down. We're hiring. And on the product side, yeah, judgment matters just as much as ever. The ability to rally a team around a vision matters just as much as ever. And design, I think, grows only more important in this world. I think in this world where software can be created more easily, design matters so much and designers matter so much. I think designers are going to be the leaders of the future and I think that more designers need to step into that leadership role and more PMs and developers and researchers also need to be willing to engage with design as well. Because I think at the end of the day that's going to be how you win or lose, and if you don't internalize that now you're going to regret it later.
turns.zh: 我认为这完全取决于事态如何发展。当然，没人知道我们是处在进步的 S 曲线还是指数曲线上，或者实际上我们处于 S 曲线的末端，但即将因为新架构的突破而变成指数增长。我认为我们唯一知道的是模型会进步。会是渐进式的吗？还是指数级的？我意思是介于两者之间。谁知道呢？但你必须相信的是，随着模型变好，你也会变好。你的组织也会随着模型变好而变好。至少目前，至少在 Figma，我们远未达到对开发需求满足的程度。我们看到了生产力提升吗？是的，温和到中等程度，但这并没有让我们想要招聘的工程人员数量下降。我们还在招人。在产品方面，判断力仍然和以往一样重要。围绕愿景凝聚团队的能力仍然和以往一样重要。而设计，我认为在这个世界中只会变得更加重要。我认为在这个软件更容易被创造的世界里，设计至关重要，设计师也至关重要。我认为设计师将成为未来的领导者，并且我认为更多的设计师需要承担起领导角色，更多的产品经理、开发者和研究人员也需要愿意参与设计。因为我认为最终这将决定你的成败，如果你现在不内化这一点，以后会后悔的。

turns.en: On the point about job displacement, there's someone who's just tweeting that OpenAI released this whole eval, GDP eval, which measures progress of AI towards replacing actual jobs, like an eval of a bunch of 40 different actual jobs, and a few of them were like the AI is like a few percentage points away from humans it turns out. And interestingly those jobs are not yet disappearing, which tells us there's hope that this may not destroy a ton of jobs. Maybe it gets to 100% and then we're screwed, but doesn't seem like it.
turns.zh: 关于工作替代的问题，有人在推特上说 OpenAI 发布了整个评估，GDP 评估，衡量 AI 在替代实际工作方面的进展，比如对 40 种不同实际工作的评估，结果发现其中一些工作 AI 离人类只有几个百分点的差距。有趣的是，这些工作还没有消失，这告诉我们有希望，这可能不会摧毁大量工作。也许它达到 100% 然后我们就完蛋了，但看起来不像。

turns.en: I mean, I think first of all, it's like evals are hard. We talked about that earlier. Secondly, the jobs don't just stay the same. They change. I think with prompting, as an engineer, there's a range of prompting abilities. The way you discretize and split up your task matters. And if you assume that a model can do more than it can do, then you're going to have a bad time. You really got to understand where its capabilities lie. And I think that changes some of the skills needed to be maximally efficient as an engineer.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，首先，评估很难。我们之前讨论过。其次，工作不会一成不变。它们会变化。我认为就提示工程而言，作为一名工程师，提示能力有高低之分。你如何分解和拆分任务很重要。如果你假设模型能做超出它能力范围的事，那你会遇到麻烦。你真的需要了解它的能力边界。我认为这改变了作为工程师需要具备的一些技能，才能达到最高效率。

turns.en: It's interesting for that survey we ran, I think it was 16 or 17% of respondents that were designers who said the developments in tech tools AI are a threat to my role. So only 17%. And I think it's pretty encouraging actually that folks understand viscerally that this is not coming for you and that the next thing will be about as tools improve, as models improve, how do you improve and adapt. There might be points where it's slow and points where it's rapid, but overall I'm quite excited. I mean, too, flex in our hiring plans. I'm going through the whole planning process on headcount right now. For the most part across the company, we're adding roles. In every conversation I'm asking about AI efficiency: what internal tools can we build to make ourselves more efficient? But also there's so much that we can do to grow. You can either see AI as an opportunity for your company to grow and do more, or you can look at it as cost-cutting efficiency. But I think the growth part is way more exciting. On the individual side, you can see it as a path for you to learn and grow and explore the world and human consciousness, or you can use it to do your homework. Obviously I've got a point of view on which one's better. So it'll be interesting to see how people adapt and grow.
turns.zh: 我们做的那项调查很有意思，我记得大约 16% 或 17% 的受访设计师表示，AI 技术工具的发展对自己的角色构成了威胁。只有 17%。我认为这实际上相当令人鼓舞，因为大家直观地理解到，AI 并不是来取代你的，接下来的重点在于，随着工具和模型的改进，你如何提升和适应。可能会有缓慢的阶段，也会有快速的阶段，但总体而言我非常兴奋。我在招聘计划上也很灵活，现在正在全面规划人员编制。公司大部分部门都在增加岗位。每次谈话我都会问及 AI 效率：我们可以构建哪些内部工具来提高效率？但同时我们也有很多增长的机会。你可以把 AI 看作公司成长和做更多事情的机会，也可以把它看作削减成本的效率工具，但我认为增长的部分更令人兴奋。对个人而言，你可以把它看作学习、成长、探索世界和人类意识的途径，也可以用它来帮你做作业。显然，我对哪种更好有自己的看法。所以，看看人们如何适应和成长会很有趣。

Obviously we have our own point of view too, so we'll have to think through it together. ts.sec: AI in daily life and work

turns.en: Before we get to our very exciting lightning round, I want to take us to AI corner. What's a way you found to use AI in your day-to-day life or work that's really interesting, maybe helpful for people to learn from? Last time we chatted, you told me about WebSim, which was this wild, crazy app that I love. Is there anything along those lines or just something you can share about AI in your life, beyond the obvious?
turns.zh: 在我们进入激动人心的闪电问答环节之前，我想进入 AI 角落。你在日常生活或工作中发现的一种使用 AI 的有趣方式是什么？也许对人们有借鉴意义。上次我们聊天时，你提到了 WebSim，那是个疯狂的应用，我很喜欢。有没有类似的东西，或者你能分享一些关于 AI 在你生活中的应用，除了那些显而易见的？

turns.en: I think there are certain domains where it does really well. I definitely often will ask an AI model about a legal question now before I call a lawyer, because I find it's not replacing my call with a great lawyer but it does inform my point of view. You have to be careful about when you do that—your conversation with AI is not the same as your conversation with the lawyer. But I think any place where you're going to consult an expert but can come in more informed, that is interesting. Another thing that's not day-to-day but I find it's very good at, and this is underexplored, is whenever you have a space of possibility and there are many dimensions to that space. So let's say I'm trying to write fiction and I want to generate a character, for example, and there are 100 personality traits that this character can have. Well, I could manually pick them from a list myself, or I can say, 'Randomly pick six out of this list of 100 and then give me for every attribute the full table of toggling that attribute positive, negative, and then all the combinations of that, and then give it a title and a description.' Now I've got a full table for those six traits, the entire possibility space of what that character sample might look like. It builds intuition about a possibility space in a different way if you do that. So that's something I think is a process that people could learn from and adapt more.
turns.zh: 我认为在某些领域它做得非常好。我现在经常在给律师打电话之前，先向 AI 模型咨询法律问题，因为我发现它并不能取代我与优秀律师的通话，但它确实能让我更有见地。不过你得小心，你和 AI 的对话与你和律师的对话是不一样的。但我认为，任何需要咨询专家但能带着更多信息去咨询的场合，都很有意思。另一件不是日常但我觉得它非常擅长、且尚未被充分探索的事情是，当你有一个可能性空间，且该空间有很多维度时。比如，假设我在写小说，想生成一个角色，这个角色可能有 100 种性格特征。我可以手动从列表中挑选，或者我说：‘从这 100 个中随机选 6 个，然后给我每个属性的完整表格，切换该属性的正面和负面，然后所有组合，再给它一个标题和描述。’这样我就得到了这 6 个特征的完整表格，即该角色样本可能呈现的整个可能性空间。这样做会以一种不同的方式建立对可能性空间的直觉。所以我认为这是一个人们可以学习并更多采用的方法。

turns.en: That is a hilarious way to talk about it. What does it taste like? Is there way some describe why it tastes so bad?
turns.zh: 这说法真搞笑。它尝起来像什么？有没有什么方式描述为什么它这么难吃？

turns.en: I mean, everything about it is gross. The smell, the texture, the um I mean, just the way it's like I mean, I Yeah, I won't go into gross details, but I really don't like chocolate.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，它的一切都很恶心。气味、质地，嗯，我的意思是，就是那种感觉，嗯，是的，我就不说恶心的细节了，但我真的不喜欢巧克力。

turns.en: That is incredible. Well, I'm not giving up um the ice cream. Lots of other desserts I like. So, just not chocolate.
turns.zh: 太不可思议了。好吧，我不会放弃冰淇淋。还有很多其他我喜欢的甜点。所以，只是不吃巧克力。

turns.en: Chocolate. Incredible. And I love that it's 0% of women don't like chocolate.
turns.zh: 巧克力。不可思议。而且我喜欢 0% 的女性不喜欢巧克力这个说法。

turns.en: I I mean, according to some random study on the internet, who knows? But yeah, it's I also have not met many women that don't like chocolate. Although, my grandmother did not like chocolate. So, yeah. I think might be genetic.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，根据网上某个随机研究，谁知道呢？但是的，我也没见过多少不喜欢巧克力的女性。不过，我祖母不喜欢巧克力。所以，是的。我认为可能是遗传的。

turns.en: There it is. Oh my god, we need 23 and me for this gene. Uh, two final questions. Where can folks find you if they want to reach out? And how can listeners be useful to you?
turns.zh: 就是这样。哦天哪，我们需要 23andMe 来检测这个基因。嗯，最后两个问题。人们如果想联系你，可以在哪里找到你？听众怎样才能对你有帮助？

turns.en: Dylan at zoink uh on X uh is one way to reach me. But if you tweet about Figma, if you share on any social media about Figma uh or write into support or post on our Figma forum uh or just talk to me at a event, I'm looking for your feedback. I'm looking to make Figma better. And I'm always trying to uh push us uh in our product to a place of excellence. So whether you want to come join the team or just want to tell us what we should do better, let me know. Along those lines, I didn't mention this, but I remember during the IPO, you were replying to people on Twitter that were complaining about Figma bugs and you were like helping them solve their Figma problem the day you were going public. One of the biggest days in your life.
turns.zh: Dylan at zoink 在 X 上是一种联系我的方式。但如果你发推文关于 Figma，如果你在任何社交媒体上分享关于 Figma 的内容，或者写信给支持团队，或者在我们的 Figma 论坛上发帖，或者只是在活动上和我聊聊，我都在寻求你的反馈。我想让 Figma 变得更好。我一直在努力推动我们的产品走向卓越。所以无论你是想加入团队，还是只是想告诉我们哪里需要改进，请告诉我。另外，我没提过这个，但我记得在 IPO 期间，你在 Twitter 上回复那些抱怨 Figma 漏洞的人，你在上市那天帮他们解决 Figma 问题。那是你人生中最重要的日子之一。

turns.en: Well, it's something I'm doing all the time and I really appreciate when people reach out and give us feedback. I see it all as a gift. So, thank you in advance. And if you have a problem that's like an actual issue, please reach out. Don't assume that we've got it all figured out. Sometimes there are rare edge cases. The broader you go, the more that you find. And we're always looking to get in touch and make sure we understand what's going on.
turns.zh: 嗯，这是我一直在做的事情，我非常感激人们主动联系并给我们反馈。我把这些都视为礼物。所以，先谢谢大家。如果你遇到了一个实际的问题，请尽管联系我们。不要以为我们已经把所有问题都解决了。有时候会有一些罕见的边缘情况。你覆盖得越广，发现的问题就越多。我们一直希望能保持联系，确保我们了解实际情况。

turns.en: Dylan, I give you a 100 out of 100 NPS score for this conversation. You're amazing. Thank you so much for doing this.
turns.zh: Dylan，这次对话我给你打 100 分的 NPS 评分。你太棒了。非常感谢你来做客。

turns.en: And bye everyone.
turns.zh: 大家再见。

turns.en: Bye. Have a good day.
turns.zh: 再见，祝你有美好的一天。

turns.en: Thank you so much for listening. If you found this valuable, you can subscribe to the show on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, or your favorite podcast app. Also, please consider giving us a rating or leaving a review as that really helps other listeners find the podcast. You can find all past episodes or learn more about the show at lennispodcast.com. See you in the next episode.
turns.zh: 非常感谢您的收听。如果您觉得本期内容有价值，可以在 Apple Podcasts、Spotify 或您喜欢的播客应用上订阅本节目。也请考虑给我们评分或留下评论，这能帮助其他听众找到这个播客。您可以在 lennispodcast.com 找到所有往期节目或了解更多信息。下期节目再见。

consensus.en: Design is the key differentiator in software; good enough is mediocre.
consensus.zh: 设计是软件的关键差异化因素；‘足够好’就是平庸。

consensus.en: Shorten time to value: get users to an awesome moment quickly.
consensus.zh: 缩短价值实现时间：让用户快速体验到产品的精彩时刻。

consensus.en: Remove blockers to adoption; fixing table stakes features improves retention.
consensus.zh: 消除采用障碍；修复基础功能可提升留存率。

consensus.en: Expand products by following the user workflow, not just TAM.
consensus.zh: 通过跟随用户工作流扩展产品，而非仅看市场规模。

consensus.en: AI will merge roles; everyone becomes a product builder with design skills.
consensus.zh: AI将融合角色；每个人都成为具备设计技能的产品构建者。

consensus.en: Taste is developed by exposure, reflection, and building a personal framework.
consensus.zh: 品味通过接触、反思和构建个人框架来培养。

contrarian.en: Market size is the wrong way to think about product expansion; follow the workflow.
contrarian.zh: 市场规模是思考产品扩展的错误方式；应跟随工作流。

contrarian.en: Make Fig Jam fun as a differentiator, not just functional.
contrarian.zh: 将Fig Jam的趣味性作为差异化因素，而不仅仅是功能性。

contrarian.en: CEOs should hire people who make them better, not just mentees.
contrarian.zh: CEO应雇佣能让自己变得更好的人，而不仅仅是受指导者。

contrarian.en: AI productivity gains are modest for established codebases, not exceptional.
contrarian.zh: 对于现有代码库，AI带来的生产力提升是温和的，并非卓越。

contrarian.en: Only 17% of designers see AI as a threat to their role.
contrarian.zh: 只有17%的设计师认为AI对自己的角色构成威胁。

contrarian.en: Chocolate is repulsive; it's likely genetic, not a matter of taste.
contrarian.zh: 巧克力令人反感；这很可能是基因决定的，而非口味问题。
